chapters.summary: Council staff gives housekeeping instructions. Chair Farah N. Louis introduces the Subcommittee on Zoning and Franchises, names attending council members, and lists the three hearings: 200 Kent Avenue rezoning, 47-03 108th Street rezoning, and Flatiron NoMad major concession. She notes the St. Augustine item will not be heard.
chapters.title: Chair Louis opens subcommittee hearing and outlines agenda

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Welcome to today's New York City Council Subcommittee on Zoning and Franchises.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this time, please silence all phones.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If you wish to speak in today's hearing, please fill out an appearance card with one of the sergeants.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Moving forward, no one is to approach the DA's chair.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to a meeting of the Subcommittee on Zoning and Franchises.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I am Councilmember Farrah Lewis, Chair of the Subcommittee.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This morning I'm joined by Councilmembers Felder, Encarnacion, Jay Sanchez, Epstein, and Remote, I believe it's Fariette.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Not yet.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Today we are holding three hearings on the following proposals.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: 200 Kent Avenue Mixed-Use Residential Rezoning and Councilmember Wrestler's District, 47-03 108th Street Mixed-Use Residential Zoning and Councilmember Thomas Henry's District, and the Flatiron Nomad

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Major Concession Application Regarding Portions of Broadway and Fifth Avenue and Councilmember Wilson and Epstein's District.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis explains the hybrid meeting format, registration process for remote and in-person testimony, two-minute time limit per witness, written testimony submission via email, and decorum rules including no video recording from the witness table.
chapters.title: Chair Louis explains hybrid format and public testimony procedures

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: One additional note about today's agenda, although the proposal known as the St.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Augustine was listed in the city record for today, this item has not been called up for council review and will not be heard.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This meeting is being held in hybrid format.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Members of the public who wish to testify may testify in person or via Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Members of the public wishing to testify remotely may register by visiting the New York City Council website at www.council.nyc.gov backslash land use to sign up.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Or for those of you here in person, please see one of the sergeant at arms to prepare.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: To prepare and submit a subject card.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Members of the public may also view a live stream broadcast of the meeting at the council's website.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: When you are called to testify before the subcommittee, if you are joining us remotely, you will remain muted until recognized by myself to speak.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: When you are recognized, your microphone will be unmuted.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: We will limit public testimony to two minutes per witness.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If you have additional testimony that you would like the subcommittee to consider, or if you have written testimony that you would like to submit instead of appearing in person, please email it to landyustestimony at council.nyc.gov.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Written testimony may be submitted up to three days after the hearing is closed.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Please indicate the LU number and or project name in the subject line of your email.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: We request the witnesses joining us remotely remain in the meeting until you are excused, as council members may have questions.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Lastly, for everyone attending the meeting today, this is a government proceeding and decorum must be observed at all times.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Members of the public are asked to not speak during the meeting unless you are testifying.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The witness table is reserved for people who are called to testify and no video recording or photography is allowed from the witness table.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Further, members of the public may not present audio or video recordings as testimony, but may submit transcripts of such recordings to the Sergeant at Arms for inclusion to the record.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Council Members Thomas Henry and Council Member Wilson.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I will now open the

chapters.summary: Chair Louis opens LU 117 regarding the Flatiron NoMad major concession application for portions of Broadway and Fifth Avenue in Council Members Wilson and Epstein's districts. She describes temporary initiatives to re-envision the corridor between 19th and 31st Streets into a safer pedestrian realm, with long-term programming to be maintained by the Flatiron NoMad Partnership.
chapters.title: Chair Louis opens Flatiron NoMad major concession hearing with context

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: the first public hearing on LU 117 regarding the Flatiron Nomad major concessions application for portions of Broadway and Fifth Avenue and Councilmember Wilson's and Epstein's districts.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Over the past few years, there have been temporary initiatives to re-envision this corridor between 19th Street and West 31st.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Into a more engaging and safer public realm for pedestrians.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This application would allow for long-term programming, which would be maintained by the Flatiron slash Nomad partnership.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: For anyone wishing to testify regarding this proposal remotely, if you have not already done so, please, you must register online by visiting the council's website at council.nyc.gov backslash land use.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: For anyone with us today, please see one of the sergeant at arms in the back to submit a speaker card.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If you would prefer to submit written testimony, you can always do so by emailing it to landyustestimony at council.nyc.gov.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I now recognize council members Wilson and Epstein for remarks.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And we've also been joined by Councilmember Fadias online.

chapters.summary: Council Member Wilson expresses excitement about the major concession for the Flatiron corridor, highlighting the opportunity to re-envision public space, integrate commercial use, and improve street safety. He thanks Council Member Epstein, DOT, the land use team, and the BID for their partnership.
chapters.title: Council Member Wilson remarks on Flatiron NoMad concession opportunity

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, and thank you to our applicant today.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm excited to hear this major concession for a big part of my district in the Flatiron.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As the Chair mentioned, this is a really great opportunity to continue how we are re-envisioning this corridor and offers a great opportunity to how we're thinking about how we're using public space.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we have a really great partner in the Business Improvement District here.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And it's going to be, in my view, if done correctly, and I think we're on the way to doing that, an opportunity for us to really think about how we are leveraging

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: public space as well as how it integrates with the commercial use, which in many ways lifts up the neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It makes for a lively and safer street.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But it also gives us an opportunity to think about how we're using public space, too, for things like our waste removal and who gets to use the public realm and for what purposes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But I think we're working really hard to strike a strong balance here that is reasonable, that gives everybody the tools that they need to make sure that this continues to be a world-class public space and how it's going to further integrate into the capital redesign of this corridor.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: look forward to this hearing and I'm looking forward to continuing to advance what I think is going to be a significant and positive change for this corridor.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I want to thank my colleague, Councilmember Epstein, who held down the fort while there was no council member for a few months.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I also want to thank

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: DOT for their partnership and our land use team, particularly Dana and Chelsea for all of the work that they have done on this.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And also the bid who I have been great stewards of this part of town.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I know we'll continue to do that once we get this over the finish line.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So thank you all very much for being here.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I'm looking forward to the hearing today.

chapters.summary: Council Member Epstein thanks the chair, Council Member Wilson, DOT, and the BID. He emphasizes community, environmental, and public values around publicly accessible space, and notes the significant responsibility the BID is taking on to maintain the space for the neighborhood.
chapters.title: Council Member Epstein remarks on Flatiron NoMad concession partnership

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I want to thank the chair for allowing us to move forward on this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I want to thank Councilmember Wilson to, you know, from day one when he started, was a real partner in this project.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And to DOT, to listening to our concerns and thoughts around how we improve this neighborhood and to make sure that what we're doing reflects our

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: community values, our environmental values, our public values around publicly facing space and making sure that people in this community could have access to a location that we think will be a vital card in our shared districts.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And finally, to the bid, we're asking a lot of you, and we appreciate you taking this on.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: It's a lot of responsibility to serve our neighborhood, a lot of responsibility to have this space be open and available to the public.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And I think this partnership will continue to grow as we continue to have this conversation.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: The DOT applicant panel is sworn in. Emily Weidenhoff, Assistant Commissioner of Public Realm at DOT, presents the major concession ULURP application with the Flatiron NoMad Partnership. She explains the expansion from 38,500 square feet to 162,345 square feet, allowing the BID to fully maintain plazas, shared streets, and pedestrian spaces, with revenue cycling back into maintenance and operations.
chapters.title: DOT's Weidenhoff presents major concession overview and expansion area

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I now call the applicant panel for this proposal, which consists of Emily Weidenhoff.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Oh, yes.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And Kyle Gorman.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Could you please raise your right hand?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you swear to tell nothing but the truth in your testimony this morning and in response to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You may begin your testimony, which is limited to 10 minutes, and you should start to conclude your presentation at about eight minutes.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: When you begin your presentation, please restate your name and your agency.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Oh, we've also been joined by Minority Leader Carr.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: My name is Emily Weidenhoff, and I'm Assistant Commissioner of Public Realm at the New York City Department of Transportation.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: We're excited to present our proposal for a major concession ULURP this morning.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: If we go to the first slide in our presentation, New York City Department of Transportation is the applicant, along with our plaza partner, the Flatiron Nomad Partnership.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: The action here today is a major concession, which pursuant to the rules of New York City, any

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: area that is over 42,000 square feet needs to go through this additional land use review.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Most recently, we've gone through a similar ULURP application for Times Square Plaza just north of here.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: This location is in Manhattan Community Board 5 in Council Districts 2 and 3.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: If we go to the next slide, you can see here the area in blue represents the existing concession agreement that we have with the Flatiron Nomad Partnership.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And this in large part represents the original public spaces that were created.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Almost 10 years ago now.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And as we have worked with the community, with our partners, with elected officials, we have worked to expand

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: pedestrian and cycling priority along the corridor.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And so the application today is really recognizing all of that transformation of street as public space.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And so we're going from the original 38,500 square feet, expanding all that additional area in red, and that's where you see additional plazas, shared streets, pedestrian space,

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: to now the application of 162,345 square feet that will essentially allow the bid to fully maintain this area.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And any of the revenue that they generate as part of their concession agreement with New York City DOT will be able to go back into the maintenance, management, and operations now of this large area.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And we feel like this is really important to give them the tools that they need to really manage this public space comprehensively in partnership with the city.

chapters.summary: Weidenhoff shows surrounding mixed-use land uses including Madison Square Park, notes the corridor is part of the Midtown South rezoning (MSMX), and explains the Broadway Vision effort to prioritize pedestrians and cyclists across five BID districts while respecting distinct neighborhoods along the corridor.
chapters.title: Weidenhoff describes surrounding land use, zoning, and Broadway Vision context

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: The next couple of slides we can run through quickly.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: First, we see the surrounding land use.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Again, a lot of mixed use residential.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: We have Madison Square Park.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: The next slide shows the zoning map.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: This portion of the corridor you'll see is part of the most recent Midtown South rezoning MSMX, which looks to bring more residential in the district.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So again, this type.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: of effort is really important as we look to continue to support a thriving, vibrant diversity of commercial, residential uses in this dense part of Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: The next slide, again, for some context, so much of this work has grown out of the Broadway Vision effort, which really looks to

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: prioritize pedestrians and cyclists along this corridor, where we have five business improvement districts.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: We are aiming to create a comprehensive, kind of coordinated experience along the corridor while still respecting the five different neighborhoods that Broadway intersects through.

chapters.summary: Weidenhoff walks through existing conditions from south to north: shared streets at 19th-20th, cycling facilities at 20th-21st, premier public spaces at 22nd-25th near the Flatiron Building with kiosks and art programming, pedestrianized blocks at 25th-28th with outdoor dining, and expanded pedestrian spaces at 28th-31st.
chapters.title: Weidenhoff presents existing conditions through the corridor

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: The next set of slides show the existing conditions through the corridor.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So we're going to quickly start in the south.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Here you see images of 19th and 20th Street.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: This is the southernmost portion of the district.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Here we have shared streets where you can see that there is still space for vehicles to pick up and drop off, but we have a lot more expanded pedestrian space for seating, for greening.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Next slide.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Here we see additional shared streets and cycling facilities.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: A big part of the Broadway vision is to create comfortable and connected two-way cycling through the district.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: This is 20th and 21st streets.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Next slide.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: We get to 22nd and 23rd Street, and this is where you see some of the larger, more premier public spaces.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: This is the public space that's adjacent to the Flatiron Building, where the bid has some of the existing subconcessions per their concession agreement.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So you see the kiosk in that middle slide there.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: This is also an area where the bid hosts a lot of art and programming.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Next slide, we move to the other big premier public space across the street from the Flatiron Building, 23rd Street up through 25th Street.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Here we have plazas as well as Worth Square and a shared street.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So part of our larger collaboration is with our colleagues in the Park Department as well as Madison Square Park Conservancy, who maintains these adjacent park spaces.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Next slide, we see the northern portion of the district, 25th to 28th Street.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: These were blocks that...

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: We're very successful as open streets with outdoor dining during the pandemic.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So part of Broadway Vision has really looked to fully pedestrianize more of these blocks to promote more outdoor dining as well as public seating.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Next slide is 28th and 29th Street.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Again, those expanded pedestrian spaces that...

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: bring safety, but also lots of opportunity for gathering, for meeting, for greening.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And the last slide is 29th through 31st Street, which is the northernmost portion of the district before we get to Herald Square.

chapters.summary: Weidenhoff describes the separate but interrelated capital street reconstruction: the southern portion (21st-27th) is in final design with community workshops planned for fall, featuring full reconstruction with utility connections. The northern portion (27th to Herald Square) is in scoping with DDC and will go through public engagement and Public Design Commission review.
chapters.title: Weidenhoff outlines long-term capital redesign plans for corridor

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Next slide.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: I wanted to give a brief overview of some of the longer term and really exciting changes coming to the corridor.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So separate from the major concession, but very interrelated, is the city's investment in a full street reconstruction and really major redesign of this corridor.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: We've been designing the kind of southern portion of the district for quite some time.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: We're now in final design and excited to get out to the community for workshops and present to the community board for the southern portion of the section later this fall.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Next slide, you can see this southern section, 21st to 27th Street, where we'll be kind of fully reconstructing the street.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: The curbs will go away, the paint and gravel will go away, and we'll have a fully transformed, greener,

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: more vibrant public space that will also have important things like utility connections, which will allow the partners to manage the space and have thriving events as well.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And then the last slide shows the future capital phases.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: 27th Street all the way up to the north, which is currently in scoping with our colleagues at the Department of Design and Construction.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And that will also go through a robust public engagement process, public workshops, community board, and ultimately the Public Design Commission for review.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: With that, I'm going to turn it over to my colleague, Kyle.

chapters.summary: Kyle Gorman from DOT's Public Realm team introduces concession types, explaining they promote active use of public spaces and create opportunities for small businesses including MWBEs. He describes the Plaza Concession Agreement allowing the BID to host commercial events, sponsorships, and sub-concessions, with agreements up to 20 years.
chapters.title: DOT's Gorman presents concession agreement overview

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: I'm Kyle Gorman from the DOT Public Realm team.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: I'm a director of programs, operations, and partnerships.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: And just lastly, we wanted to highlight a little bit more about what concessions actually look like on these plazas.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: So we move to two slides ahead, please.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: I'll give a little bit of a brief overview of concessions.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: So these promote the positive and active use of these spaces, creates opportunities for small businesses, especially NWBEs.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: There's a few different types of concession agreements that DOT does.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: Predominantly today, we're really talking about the Plaza Concession Agreement, which allows the bid to host commercial events and derive fees from those commercial entities that are hosting those events, entering into sponsorship agreements and having sub-concessions, which we'll talk about briefly.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: Typically, these are multi-year agreements that can be up to 20 years, which was something that we're looking to pursue with the bid.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

chapters.summary: Gorman explains commercial events coordinated with SAPO, where entities like Nike or Netflix host single-day marketing events with fee schedules. He also describes limited sponsorship opportunities, clarifying these are not advertising but small branding callouts with specific parameters for logos and branding in the public realm.
chapters.title: Gorman details commercial events and sponsorship concession types

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: So getting into the specific types of concessions, the first is commercial events.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: These are done in collaboration with the Street Activity Permit Office, SAPO, who collects a fee.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: The city has a fee schedule for the different types of events.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: It can happen in these spaces when these commercial entities want to host these different types of sort of marketing opportunities for themselves.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: And our plaza partners also get to collect a fee based on a similar type of schedule that DOT develops in coordination with the bid.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: These are, you know, sometimes Nike or Netflix or what have you are able to host these usually single day events to promote some sort of a new initiative or marketing opportunity for that particular business.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: We also allow for a limited amount of sponsorship.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: It is in no ways to be considered advertising.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: It's more of just a way to give a sort of small call out to a business or some type of organization that's able to sponsor some element.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: We have very specific parameters and requirements for what this looks like in the public realm.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: And usually it is relegated to logos and branding.

chapters.summary: Gorman covers kiosk structures for food and merchandise sales, noting Flatiron has operated a kiosk for years. He describes sub-concessions primarily as tent-and-table style markets for seasonal or year-round use, including farmer's markets, coordinated with multiple city agencies depending on the market type.
chapters.title: Gorman describes kiosks, sub-concessions, and market types

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: Next slide.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: In a number of our plazas, we have structures that are kiosks that allow for the sales of food and other types of merchandise.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: Flatiron has, for a number of years, operated a kiosk themselves as well.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: This is a more formal structure for a small business to sell food and beverage or small types of merch.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: We have unique types of kiosks that we've seen in our public spaces across the years.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: DOT itself also has a standard design that we incorporate in the capital build-out of these spaces, which will be contemplated in the capital project that Emily had mentioned as well.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: Next slide.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: Through our

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: Our sub-concessions, typically sub-concessions come by way of markets, but you can enter into quite a number of different types of sub-concessions.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: But the most predominant one is a tent and table style market that usually includes some sale of merch

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: or food, typically on a seasonal basis, maybe around the holidays or more year long when it comes like around for a farmer's market and connecting folks to local fresh food and locally made goods.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: This is done in coordination with quite a number of agencies, especially depending on the different types of market concessions that will take place.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: But this is another type of activation that we have done through our concessions over the years.

chapters.summary: Gorman describes pairing concession opportunities with public benefits like programming alongside small business revenue. He introduces concession seating as DOT's form of outdoor dining on plazas, distinct from the Dining Out program, allowing up to 33% of plaza area for concession seating, or up to 50% through a major concession ULURP as proposed today.
chapters.title: Gorman explains public benefits pairing and concession seating

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: Next slide.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: A lot of times we try to pair these concession opportunities with some sort of public benefits.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: So we have a lot of great use cases around the city where we have a sub-concession, like let's say a small kiosk that's selling food and beverage

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: that's paired with some sort of event or programming that still provides this public benefit in the space while still allowing a small local business to make some sort of revenue as well as creating revenue stream for the bid.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: And then just the last two slides, because I know we're almost at time, talking about one of our newest types of sub-concessions is concession seating.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: This is our form of outdoor dining on plazas through our concession agreements.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: It should be noted that this is separate and distinct from the Dining Out program, which is the general outdoor dining program in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: We allow for typically up to 33% of a plaza.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: to occupy concession seating for those businesses.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: But when we go through a major concession, ULURP, as we're talking about today, that can be up to 50%.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: That is something we've talked very closely with the council members about and how exactly we would operationalize that in coordination with the bid and DOT and its other agencies.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: So we feel very sort of strongly about maintaining this, but also maintaining the public space benefit of these concession seating opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: And just last slide, you can just get a little visualization of what concession seating looks like down the line.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: We're happy to talk about that more.

utterances.speaker: Kyle Gorman
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis asks three questions: who maintains the pedestrian features (Flatiron NoMad Partnership), how frequently DOT inspects the concession area (quarterly reports, audited financial statements, site visits for new installations and 311 requests), and whether DOT requires annual public reporting of revenues and expenditures (reviewed internally but not posted online).
chapters.title: Chair Louis questions DOT on maintenance, oversight, and revenue reporting

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I have three quick questions before I hand it over to Councilmember Wilson and Epstein.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Who has been maintaining the pedestrian-oriented features along this stretch of Broadway?

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: That's been the Flatiron Nomad Partnership.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: How frequently will DOT inspect the concession area, and what oversight will DOT maintain once the agreement is executed?

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Great question.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So as part of DOT's regular concession process, we require that our concession partners provide quarterly reports.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And we also have some requirements for audited financial statements.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So there's a team at DOT in our concessions division who's reviewing that on a regular basis.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: We also have teams that are out and about on the corridor that are either

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: because we are organizing a meeting with the bid or we've received some public questions or like a 311 request.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: There's a kind of whole host of different reasons why DOT staff would be out on site, you know, looking at various things in the plaza.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: We don't have a, you know, an exact regimented process, but anytime anything new is installed,

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: we not only review internally first, but then we will do a site visit to look at the installation and confirm it meets.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: the proposal.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you for that response.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And does DOT require annual public reporting showing revenues collected and how do those funds get spent in major concession areas?

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So as part of again the requirement for our concession agreement that's exactly what we are looking at is all of the revenue coming in and then how it's going to various

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: allowed expenses per the terms of the concession agreement.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: That's not something we post online, but it is something that is, you know, part of, yes.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Council members Epstein and Wilson, whoever wants to go first.

chapters.summary: Epstein asks about DOT's role in overseeing environmental agreements. Weidenhoff confirms the agreement will require partners and subconcessionaires to follow environmental laws, and DOT will promote recycling and composting. Epstein asks if the BID will report on environmental compliance; Weidenhoff says specifics will be in the agreement and ongoing conversations, with DOT as backup oversight entity.
chapters.title: Council Member Epstein questions DOT on environmental practices oversight

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you, and thank you for spending time with us as we go through these questions and issues.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I think you and your team have been really, really helpful, so I appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So I think we talked a lot about kind of environmental issues, and I think we're all in the same place around trying to become more environmentally friendly.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: What role will DOT have in overseeing whatever agreements we come to in the end?

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So we've talked with your office about, you know, working in certainly making sure that as part of the agreement itself, that we're being explicit that all of

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: both our partner and its subconcessionaires need to follow all relevant rules related to environmental law.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: We're also committed to, where possible, promoting recycling, composting, and other environmental-friendly practices.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And that's something, again, we'll coordinate closely with the bid on to make sure that where feasible, that's playing out in all the activities.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And is the bid going to report to you on those issues in your agreement with the bid?

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So again, there will be some specificity in the agreement itself, but a lot happens outside the agreement in terms of RFP proposals.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: things like that.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So that's something we'll be in conversation with them about.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And certainly we know it is a really important value here, but also honestly citywide.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So it's something that, yes, we will be making sure we do to the extent possible environmentally safe practices and operations.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And so if there was an issue, obviously we can go to the bid, but you'll be a backup entity.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: DOT is very committed to, you know, we are the long-term stewards of these public spaces and happy to engage with you at any point.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Yes.

chapters.summary: Epstein asks about DOT's role in ensuring public seating remains available and visible. Weidenhoff says DOT reviews and approves proposals balancing public and concession seating, regularly checks that approved setups are in place, and ensures concession seating does not encroach into open public seating areas.
chapters.title: Council Member Epstein questions DOT on public seating oversight

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And just on the public seating, we've spent a lot of time talking about making sure that it's there.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: People know it's public seating.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: It's available.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: What role would DOT play in ensuring that that is happening and making sure that there's notices and information up for the public that they are aware of it?

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Yeah, so that public seating is incredibly important to us.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: That's all of these spaces are public space.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So we will be not only kind of reviewing and approving the proposals that look at the balance of the public seating as well as the concession seating.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And that will be something that we're kind of evaluating as these spaces are evolving.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: The life, the use of these spaces changes a lot over the years.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So we don't see it as being static.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: It'll be something we care deeply about and we'll be evaluating.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: But also.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So we will be regularly checking in to make sure that the setups that are kind of reviewed and approved per the subconcessions are actually in place.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: They're not kind of encroaching and creeping into the other spaces that are for open, accessible public seating.

chapters.summary: Epstein asks about DOT's commitment to long-term public improvements including water fountains and a public bathroom. Weidenhoff says DOT is working with DDC to integrate drinking fountains and water bottle filling stations into capital design, and is in conversations with sibling agencies about providing a public restroom, though technical constraints exist.
chapters.title: Council Member Epstein questions DOT on water fountains and public restrooms

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I know we talked about long-term public improvements.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We talked about putting in publicly available water fountains, putting in a public bathroom.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And then I know this is a little further down the pike.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm wondering kind of what DOT's commitment into those public improvements are and where you see this happening in the context of the work that we're doing in the area.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Those are all important elements for a healthy, thriving space.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And so we are working with our colleagues at Department of Design and Construction to integrate public drinking fountains, water bottle filling stations into the capital design.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And we're currently in conversations with our colleagues in a few city agencies about the ability to provide a public restroom.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So we'll be exploring those elements.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: There are a lot of technical constraints to those, but things that we're in conversation with sibling agencies about.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yeah, I just want to make sure the DOT is committed to trying to make that happen.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

chapters.summary: Wilson asks how the final capital design will physically protect pedestrians from the two-way bike lane, per Community Board 5's recommendation. Weidenhoff describes plantings between cycling and pedestrian space, tactile surfaces, tactile domes at crossings, and lowered cycling lanes in some blocks. Wilson follows up on whether the BID is consulted in design; Weidenhoff confirms active collaboration.
chapters.title: Council Member Wilson questions DOT on pedestrian-bike separation and BID design involvement

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'd also like to echo Councilman Epstein's comments about the collaboration and responsiveness from DOT.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know it's been a lot of back and forth, but appreciate you all being at the table the whole time in really good faith.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I have a couple of questions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So in their recommendations, Community Board 5 emphasized the importance of permanent separation between pedestrian areas and the proposed two-way bike lane.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Can DOT explain how the final capital design will physically protect pedestrians?

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So we're still in design, and we'll be sharing a lot of these details with the public and when we go back to the community board.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: But one of the great things about the capital design is we have a lot more tools that we're able to use versus just the paint and gravel.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So there are a few different elements that we're looking at.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: One is just actually designing more planting in between the two-way cycling and the pedestrian space so it'll naturally feel separated.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: We're also looking at tactile surfaces, so a little bit of a raised

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: bump, if you will, that will help both kind of visually but also people

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: with visual impairments to feel the difference between the cycle track and the pedestrian space, as well as the tactile.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: domes where we have an indicate a physical crossing.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So those are some of the elements that we're looking to incorporate into the final design so it'll feel more separated.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: In some of the blocks, we're actually lowering the cycling lane a little bit lower than the sidewalk grade.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So again, there will be a little bit of a physical distinction kind of clarifying who uses which space.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Is the business improvement district part of the...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Consulted during this design.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If we're talking about plantings, I imagine they're going to be taking care of those.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They're part of that design process.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: We have a big meeting this week to dig into some of those details.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: But yes, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Our design process is heavily reflective of the reality that our plaza partner,

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: are responsible for the long-term maintenance and management of the spaces, and we want to make sure we're designing both to a really world-class public standard, but also one that our partners can maintain and sustain.

chapters.summary: Wilson asks about Community Board 5's recommendation to relocate the Citi Bike station between 29th and 30th Streets. Weidenhoff says DOT is discussing layouts with bike share colleagues, with proposals for the southern portion coming to the public soon, and the northern portion in the next design phase.
chapters.title: Council Member Wilson questions DOT on Citi Bike station relocation

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Community Board 5 also recommended relocating the city bike station between 29th and 30th to improve the pedestrian circulation.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Has DOT evaluated that recommendation and what factors will determine whether or not that station is relocated?

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So we are discussing with our bike share colleagues at DOT different layouts for City Bike with the capital design.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: We have a few proposals that we'll be sharing with the public as part of the southern portion.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: That northern portion that you're mentioning, 29 to 30, that's in our next phase of design.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So we're not there yet.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: But as we look to kind of fully reimagine the street, we'll be looking at kind of how all the different pieces fit together and have a kind of balanced, cohesive design.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And City Bike will be one of those elements that we're balancing.

chapters.summary: Wilson asks for an update on the Midtown South mixed-use commitments for a kid-first district between 22nd and 25th Streets and two additional car-free blocks. Weidenhoff says DOT is exploring additional pedestrianization proposals for public workshops and is in planning stages for the kid-friendly proposal with BID colleagues.
chapters.title: Council Member Wilson questions DOT on kid-first district and car-free blocks commitments

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And then my last question, as part of the Midtown South mixed-use points of agreement, the administration committed to advancing a

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: kid-first district between West 22nd and West 25th, as well as creating two additional car-free blocks.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Can you provide an update on the status of these commitments?

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So we are looking at additional opportunities to pedestrianize blocks of Broadway, and those will be...

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: proposals that we bring back to the public in the workshop stage of our capital redesign.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So more to come on that soon.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And then our team is working on the kind of kid-friendly proposal.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: We're currently in the planning stages with our bid colleagues along the corridor and hope to have more to circle back on that soon.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Council.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Council are then members of the public who wish to testify remotely or in person regarding this proposal.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: No, but we do have a potentially.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: No?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: No questions?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You sure?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, for asking.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: No, we do have, let me start with, we do not have members of the public who want to testify in person or online, but however, Council Member Felder, I believe, has a question.

chapters.summary: Felder asks whether DOT regularly analyzes Citi Bike usage to remove underused bikes, observing unused bikes in his district and across the city. Weidenhoff notes her team is not responsible for Citi Bike but says there is data on every bike and dock, and offers to connect Felder with the relevant DOT team for more information.
chapters.title: Council Member Felder questions DOT on Citi Bike usage analysis

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I have two questions of a general nature.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: Number one about the city bike, the program that's been around for many, many years.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: Does DOT do a regular analysis to see the usage or not of those bikes?

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And then...

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: For example, if after, I don't know, a year or two,

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: you have 20 bikes that have been sitting there for who knows, and one bike a week is being used, removing the bike, those bikes.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And the reason I ask is that I see in my own district and I see it all over the city.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: There are certainly it's used in many places, but there are other places that it's just not for whatever reason.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And unless this is some sort of cultural art exhibit.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: That the city has.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: over about city bikes that are not used.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I'd like to, can you respond to that?

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So our team is not responsible for City Bike, but we appreciate your question.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: I can say that there is a big team at DOT.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Every single City Bike, there is data about where that bike is and the different docks.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And so we are happy to go back and have them provide you with a little bit more information about that.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I would appreciate that very much.

chapters.summary: Felder expresses frustration that the city does not mandate helmets for cyclists using city streets, comparing it to seatbelt laws. He asks DOT to follow up on why there is no helmet requirement. Weidenhoff notes DOT's commitment to safety and free helmet distribution programs. Felder clarifies he wants mandatory helmet use, not free helmets.
chapters.title: Council Member Felder questions DOT on bicycle helmet requirements

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And the second question is.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: About helmets.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I don't understand it.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: You do not have a program, when I say you, DOT.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I mean, I remember distinctly when they started obligating drivers to use seatbelts.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: Because of safety.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I'm very familiar with, for whatever reason, with the

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: accidents or whatever that occur with bicyclists or, you know, that do not wear helmets.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: To the bicyclists and to the people that, unfortunately, if they bump into them, you know, that's that.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: So I don't get the whole thing.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I want to understand.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And this is not to you.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: You can send this to your friends there as well.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I do not understand how the city functions without having a, I don't know what you want to call it, one street, one rule about safety.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: You know, I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I know that my kids, I'm not the disciplinarian, never was.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I always used to say, the kids asked me, I said, I used to say, it's okay with me, but ask mommy.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And then she'd kill me.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: But a helmet, you could not get on a bike, you know, without the helmet.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: You know, my wife.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And you train the kids from an early time.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And then they...

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: Get older, and instead of it continuing, you say, okay, now it's okay, you know.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And the city has no program of obligating people who are using city streets.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: You know, I understand if the guy wants to drive around his alley, you know, in circles without a helmet.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: But they're using city streets without helmets, and that's been going on for years.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And to top it all off, your city, you know, the program that you have, the bicycle program in the city, I'm almost done.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: Yeah, I did not.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I had a fight with my wife this morning.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: There is no program to ensure the safety of the bicyclists by obligating anyone using a city street, whether it's on a bicycle

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: or anything else, certainly.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: If you're using a city street, just like you buckle up, you have to have a helmet.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I don't understand it.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: So you can have your friends at DOT get back to me on that as well if I'd appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: Yeah, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And DOT cares incredibly deeply about safety.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: And we do have a lot of amazing teams that are partnering with elected officials to hand out free helmets to people all across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Emily Weidenhoff
utterances.text: So we'd be happy to connect you with them.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I don't want a free helmet.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I want everyone to have to wear one.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis calls for public testimony three times; no members of the public sign up in person or online. The hearing on LU 117 is closed and items are laid over. The DOT panel is excused.
chapters.title: Flatiron NoMad hearing closed: no public testimony, items laid over

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you for your response.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Council Members Salam and Ressler.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Stand at ease.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If there are any members of the public who wish to testify regarding this proposal remotely, please press the raise hand button now.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If you are in person, please identify yourself to one of the sergeant at arms.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This is the last call.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You have 30 seconds.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Being there are no members of the public who wish to testify regarding LU 117 relating to the Flatiron Nomad major concessions application, this public hearing is now closed.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The items are laid over.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This panel is excused.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you for being here today.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis opens LUs 118 and 119 regarding the 47-03 108th Street mixed-use residential rezoning in Corona, Queens, in Council Member Thomas-Henry's district. The proposal involves a zoning map and MIH text amendment for approximately 119 apartments, with 30 permanently affordable under MIH Option 1.
chapters.title: Chair Louis opens 47-03 108th Street rezoning hearing

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I now open the second public hearing on LUs 118 and 119 regarding 47-03 108th Street, a mixed-use residential rezoning proposal in Corona, Queens, in Councilmember Thomas Henry's district.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The proposal involves a zoning map and an MIH zoning tax amendment.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Applicant is seeking to develop a mixed-use housing development with approximately 119 apartments.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Mandatory inclusionary housing would be mapped as part of this application, which would require 30 apartments to be permanently affordable under MIH Option 1.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: For anyone wishing to testify regarding this proposal remotely, if you have not already done so, you must register online by visiting the Council's website at council.nyc.gov backslash land use.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: For anyone with us in person, please see one of the sergeant at arms to submit a speaker card.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If you would prefer to submit written testimony, you can always do so by email.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: By emailing landusestestimonyatcouncil.nyc.gov, I now recognize Councilman Thomas Henry.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: She doesn't have remarks.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I will now call the applicant panel for this proposal, which consists of Frank St.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Jacques and Andrew Esposito.

chapters.summary: The applicant panel is sworn in. Frank St. Jacques of Ackerman LLP presents the application to establish an R7X/C2-4 zoning district on 108th Street in Corona, facilitating a 13-story mixed-use development with approximately 119 apartments. He describes the site's R6B zoning from 1989, adjacency to R5 districts, and transit-oriented location near the 7 train.
chapters.title: Applicant St. Jacques presents 108th Street zoning overview and site context

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Could you please raise your right hand?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you swear to tell the truth and nothing but the truth in your testimony this morning and in response to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You may begin your testimony, which is limited to 10 minutes, and you should start.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You should start to conclude at eight minutes.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: When you begin your presentation, please restate your name and the name of your organization.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lewis and council members.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: My name is Frank Sanjok.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: I'm with Ackerman LLP.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: We're land use counsel for the applicant, and I'm joined by Andrew Esposito representing ownership.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: This is an application in Queens Community District 4 to establish an R7X C24 zoning district that would facilitate the construction of a 13-story mixed-use development with approximately 119

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: apartments on 108th Street in Corona.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: As shown in the zoning map on the screen, the rezoning area is on the east side of 108th Street, which is a wide north-south corridor through the area.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: And the rezoning area is located between 47th Avenue to the north and 48th Avenue to the south.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: It was zoned R6B in the 1989 Elmhurst Corona rezoning.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: And the R6B is adjacent to R5 zoning districts.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: This land use map shows that the rezoning area and the block to the north are occupied by non-conforming industrial uses.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: There's no commercial overlay and industrial uses are not permitted in residential zoning districts.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: And 108th Street has a mix of non-conforming commercial and industrial uses.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: The rezoning area is transit oriented with bus access along 108th Street and Corona Avenue to the west.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: And the 7 train is approximately a half mile to the north of the rezoning area.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: And as you can see, the mid blocks beyond 108th Street are generally lower density residential.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: And there are some community facility uses and the park is just a block to the east.

chapters.summary: St. Jacques shows the rezoning area occupied by non-conforming auto-related uses on 108th Street, with lower-density residential mid-blocks to the east. He explains the proposed R7X envelope as medium-density appropriate for the transit-oriented wide corridor block front, with C2-4 allowing local retail and service uses on the historically mixed-use stretch.
chapters.title: St. Jacques describes existing conditions and proposed R7X/C2-4 zoning

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: So this photograph facing east, you can see the rezoning area.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Again, it's just that block front on 108th Street between 47th and 48th.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: And it is occupied by non-conforming auto-related uses.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: And then you can see some of that surrounding residential context.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Generally lower density, but multifamily row houses.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: There's a five-story basement multifamily building immediately to the east of the site along 48th Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: The proposed R7X envelope is a medium density residential zoning district, which is appropriate at this transit oriented site, which is on the block front of a wide commercial corridor.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: The C24 would allow local commercial uses, retail and service uses on this historically

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: mixed use stretch of 108th Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: The project details are shown here.

chapters.summary: St. Jacques details the 13-story, 120,000-square-foot mixed-use building with 119 total apartments, 30 permanently affordable under MIH Option 1 (25% of residential floor area at 60% AMI weighted average, with 10% at 40% AMI). He notes Community District 4 has the sixth-highest rent burden in NYC at 54%, above Queens and citywide averages.
chapters.title: St. Jacques presents project details and MIH affordability breakdown

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Again, this is a mixed-use building rising to 13 stories.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: The R7X permits a floor area ratio, or FAR, of 6.0.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: So the full development would be about 120,000 square feet with commercial and community facility space, as well as parking.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: I'll note that the parking required here would be for about 13 spaces, but recognizing a demand in the area, we're providing about three times as much.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: There will be a set aside for mandatory inclusionary housing.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: which is shown on this slide here.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: The proposed development has, again, 119 total apartments, 30 of which would be permanently affordable under MIH option one, which requires a set aside of 25% of the residential floor area

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: at a weighted average of 60% AMI with 10% at 40% AMI.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: This results in 12 units at 40% AMI and 80% AMI and six at 60% AMI, representing a spectrum of affordability within this district.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Community District 4 has a critical need for housing.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: We're 54% residents are rent burdened.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: This is the sixth highest rent burden population in New York City and the second highest in Queens, higher than Queens overall at 46% and New York City overall at 44.5%.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: So we believe that the introduction of new housing, investment and new development at the site with the inclusion of mandatory inclusionary housing would be appropriate and we welcome any questions.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Louis asks why R7X was selected given a recent R7A approval three blocks south. St. Jacques explains the site is entirely on the 108th Street block front, closer to transit, with fewer lower-density homes within the block, making R7X more appropriate due to separation from lower-density context.
chapters.title: Chair Louis questions applicant on R7X zoning selection rationale

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I have a couple of questions and I'll hand it over to Councilmember Thomas Henry.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Last week, we approved an application zoned as R7A for development three blocks south of this site.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: How was this R7X district selected and why is R7X appropriate for this site?

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: So referring to the 5020 108th Street rezoning just a few blocks to the south, this site we looked at R7X because it's entirely on 108th Street, the block front,

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: 200 by 100 feet along 108th Street.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: It's closer to transit resources and the context.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Different from the project to the south at 5020 108th Street, there are fewer lower density homes, you know, sort of within the block where the rezoning is contemplated.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: So we believe that the separation created by virtue of this rezoning area and project site being on the block front made the R7X more appropriate.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Louis asks for the unit breakdown including affordability levels and bedroom mix. St. Jacques says the building has approximately 60% one-bedroom and 40% two-bedroom units, with the same mix reflected in the MIH affordable units.
chapters.title: Chair Louis questions applicant on unit breakdown and bedroom mix

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And can you share your current proposed breakdown of units for the development?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I know you shared some earlier, but including affordability levels and the mix of unit sizes.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: If we could just go back one slide, that should provide the affordability breakdown.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: And then as far as the...

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: The total breakdown, this is a building that the initial illustrative design has only one bedroom and two bedroom units.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: So it's approximately 60% one bedrooms and 40% two bedrooms.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: That mix is reflected in the MIH breakdown as well.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I'll now recognize Councilmember Thomas Henry.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks whether the applicant has considered three-bedroom units or more two-bedrooms; St. Jacques says they hadn't but are happy to look into it. She asks about using MIH Option 3 for lower AMI bands; St. Jacques notes Option 1 allows 25% affordable while Option 3 allows only 20%, resulting in fewer affordable units.
chapters.title: Council Member Thomas-Henry questions applicant on bedroom mix and MIH Option 3

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: A couple of questions.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: To piggyback on Councilmember Lewis'question about the bedroom mix, have you considered any three bedrooms or a larger number of two bedrooms versus the one bedroom?

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: That's something we hadn't considered, but we're happy to look into.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So Community Board 4 has expressed concerns, as you can imagine, about the affordability within the proposed development.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: While there is a wide...

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Right away of MIH options.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Have you considered using MIH option three to reach lower AMI bands?

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: This is, again, something we hadn't considered, but we're happy to look into.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: There were also quite a few recommendations that came from the borough president.
