utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Have you been able to review those and are those some things that you're in line with?

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: I'll just jump back to the MIH option three question quickly.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: I just will note that, as you're aware, the MIH option one allows for a 25%

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: set aside for affordability, there would be fewer affordable units by using MIH option three.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: I think I just said that backwards.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: MIH option one would allow for 25%, while MIH option three would allow for only 20%.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: So it would result in fewer affordable units.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: So that was one aspect of thinking about.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Using MIH option one.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: And then with respect to the president's conditions.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry raises the borough president's recommendations including 30% MWBE hiring goal with quarterly reporting; St. Jacques has no objection. She asks about community facility and commercial space plans; St. Jacques says they've had initial discussions with Common Point but welcome council member input. She asks about investing in local parks; St. Jacobs says they're not opposed but need to understand the mechanism.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry questions applicant on borough president conditions, MWBE, community facility, and park investment

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And I can highlight a couple of them that again are of great importance to the community as well.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Utilizing local residents for working on the construction of this project, minimum of 30% MWBE goal.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: With quarterly reporting on hiring initiatives to both the borough president's office and my office.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: So we have no objection to that condition.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And also, what are you considering for the community facility spaces as well as the commercial spaces?

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: So we've had some conversation with your office.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: At this point, we have some ideas about potential tenants for the community facility.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: We'd like to continue that conversation with input and your feedback.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: We have had initial discussion with Common Point, but not selected a specific user.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: for this space.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: So we're happy to continue that conversation and look forward to having some feedback from you as to an appropriate community facility tenant.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And as you can imagine, there are a lot of families in this area and we are in desperate need of park space.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Would you guys be open to, excuse me, investing in the local parks?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: For any improvements needed.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: We haven't sort of, we don't sort of fully understand the, to generally answer your question, we're not opposed to that.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: We're just concerned with what that would look like and, you know, how that would work in the context of seeking a discretionary action before the city council.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Sorry about that.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry confirms the building is all-electric; St. Jacques notes sustainability and resiliency features and no objection to the borough president's conditions for pervious pavement, bioswales, and rain gardens. Thomas-Henry raises concerns about the building being twice the height of anything in the district; St. Jacques says they've explored design changes to lower the height and will share them.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry questions applicant on sustainability features and building height concerns

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And then last, I did see it is an all-electric building.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: That's correct.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: And so, you know, along those lines, the building will have a number of sustainability features as well as resiliency features built in.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: So we have no objection to the borough president's condition to incorporate pervious payment, bioswales, and rain gardens.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: We understand that DOT has some new programs relating to bioswales and rain gardens and tree pits.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: So we're happy to look into that as well.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Definitely, because we've had quite a few flooding issues, so that would be greatly appreciated.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And last but not least, and we've discussed this, I do have concerns over the height.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: This literally would be the tallest thing in this part of the district by twice the size.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: We understand, Councilmember, and we have looked into some potential design changes that would lower the height.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: We're happy to share those with you moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I appreciate it, and I appreciate for your constant communication and working with my office on this project.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Council, are there, well, I now invite my, any colleagues have questions?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Councilman Encarnacion.

chapters.summary: Encarnación notes the building looks out of character and the community board was not in favor, asking about direct neighborhood engagement beyond community board meetings. St. Jacques says they only engaged through the ULURP process and community board hearings. Andrew Esposito adds he has independent relationships with CB4 members from prior projects and has had conversations about height concerns.
chapters.title: Council Member Encarnación questions applicant on community engagement

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: So I agree.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: I think it, seeing that it looks very out of character for what the neighborhood currently looks like now, and the fact that the community board has

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: not necessarily been in favor of this.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Can you outline some of the community engagement you've had with some of the local neighbors there?

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: If there's been any opportunities to meet with them, to talk them through what are their needs, what would they like to see out of the project outside of the community board meetings?

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Outside of the community board meetings, no, we availed ourselves to the Euler process.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: We met several times with the land use committee and then had hearings with the land use committee and the full community board.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: So no, with the direct neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: No outside engagement.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: I think, you know, Andrew has a relationship with some of the council members and, you know, has spoken to them about specific or sorry, excuse me, the community board members and, you know, had some offline conversation, but not any real direct engagement with the community.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Esposito
utterances.text: So I do have an independent relationship with some of the members of Community Board 4.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Esposito
utterances.text: We've had some very spirited, I wouldn't even call them debates, conversations stemming from an earlier project at 7801 Queens Boulevard, where we worked with...

utterances.speaker: Andrew Esposito
utterances.text: Councilmember Krishnan's office, in addition to CB4, and we've been able to achieve some creative results that I think they're relatively pleased with.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Esposito
utterances.text: So we have had, Frank, even to the extent you may not be privy to, conversations about how to, you know, certainly hear them, work with them.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Esposito
utterances.text: Height has been a significant component.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Esposito
utterances.text: So I can tell you that for what that's worth to add, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I'm going to kick it back.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Oh, you have another one?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I'm done.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I'm going to kick it back to Thomas Henry.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks if the applicant is open to collaborating with other developers along the corridor on design and commercial space to preserve neighborhood character and avoid retail monopolization. St. Jacques confirms his office represented the nearby development and coordination is easy. Esposito adds they have a personal relationship with that developer and agree coordination on community facility uses would be smart.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry questions applicant on coordination with other corridor developers

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Just one more question.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: As you heard Chair Lewis mention, there was another development recently approved in this corridor a couple of blocks down, and there are potential other developments along this.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: You're open to collaborating with the other developers just in terms of design and when you're thinking about the commercial space.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Some type of coordination so that all developments are working cohesively with each other in terms of the character of the neighborhood, preserving the character of the neighborhood, as well as not looking to monopolize the retail.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: And to be perfectly clear, my office represented the other developments.

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: So establishing that coordination, you know,

utterances.speaker: Frank St. Jacques
utterances.text: It's easy to do, and we're happy to keep your office looped in on that.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Esposito
utterances.text: And we have a personal relationship with that owner and developer as well, and we've spoken preliminarily, but I think that's actually a really great idea.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Esposito
utterances.text: I'm also thinking in terms of the community facility, for example.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Esposito
utterances.text: to the extent they're going to have any.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Esposito
utterances.text: We don't need the same services, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Esposito
utterances.text: So I think if we all speak on that point, that would be smart too.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Yep, we're all on the same page.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: No problem.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis calls for public testimony three times; no members of the public sign up in person or online. The hearing on LUs 118 and 119 is closed and items are laid over. The applicant panel is excused.
chapters.title: 108th Street hearing closed: no public testimony, items laid over

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Council, are there any members of the public who wish to testify remotely or in person regarding this proposal?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Currently in the signers, no members who have signed up online or in person.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If there are any members of the public who wish to testify regarding this proposal remotely, please press the raise hand button now.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If you are in person, please identify yourself to one of the sergeant at arms.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This is the last call.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Being that there are no members of the public who wish to testify regarding LUs 118 and 119 regarding the 47-03-108 mixed-use residential rezoning proposal, this public hearing is now closed.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The items are laid over.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You are now excused.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you for being here.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis opens LUs 115 and 116 regarding the 200 Kent Avenue mixed-use residential rezoning along the Williamsburg waterfront in Council Member Ressler's district. The proposal involves a zoning map and MIH text amendment to enlarge an existing commercial building into approximately 143 apartments, with 36 permanently affordable under MIH Option 1.
chapters.title: Chair Louis opens 200 Kent Avenue rezoning hearing

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And we will now open our last public hearing, third public hearing, on LUs 115 and 116 regarding 200 Kent Avenue,

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: a mixed-use residential rezoning proposal along the Williamsburg waterfront in Brooklyn and Councilmember Ressler's district.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The proposal involves a zoning map and an MIH zoning text amendment.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Applicant is seeking to enlarge an existing commercial building into a mixed-use housing development with approximately 143 apartments.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Mandatory inclusionary housing would be mapped as part of this application, which would require 36 apartments to be permanently afforded.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: under MIH option one.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: For anyone wishing to testify regarding this proposal remotely, if you have not already done so, you must register online by visiting the council's website at council.nyc.gov backslash land use.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: For anyone with us in person, please see one of the sergeant at arms to submit a speaker card.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If you would prefer to submit written testimony, you can also do so by emailing it to land use testimony at council.nyc.gov.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Councilmember Ressler had to step out.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I will now call the applicant panel for this proposal, which consists of Jeremy Cozen and Julio Margarino.

chapters.summary: The applicant panel is sworn in. Jeremy Cozen of Freedman LLP presents two actions: a zoning map amendment from M1-4 to M1-4A/R7X mixed-use district and establishing an MIH area, resulting in approximately 143 dwelling units with 29-43 affordable depending on MIH option. He notes Brooklyn CB1 and the Borough President approved with conditions, and City Planning Commission approved unanimously.
chapters.title: Applicant Cozen presents 200 Kent Avenue rezoning overview and application status

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Still good morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Can you please raise your right hand?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Sir, can you please raise your hand?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you swear to tell the truth and nothing but the truth in your testimony this morning and in response to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You may now begin your testimony, which is limited to 10 minutes.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Please start wrapping at 8 minutes when you begin your presentation.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Please restate your name and the name of your organization.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: I think good afternoon now, Chair and members of the subcommittee.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Jeremy Cozen, Freed Frank Harris Shriver Jacobson LLP, Land Use Council for the Application, the 200 Kent Avenue Rezoning.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: We are here today to discuss two proposed actions.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: The first is a zoning map amendment to rezone the project area from an M14 light manufacturing district to an M14AR7X mixed-use district.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: The second action is to map this area, the area that will be rezoned, to establish an MIH area.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: This would result in approximately 143 dwelling units, 29 to 43 of which would be affordable depending on the MIH option selected.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: A brief overview of the status of this application is, I think, warranted at this point.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: The Brooklyn Community Board 1 voted favorably with conditions, as well as the Brooklyn Borough President.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Those conditions related mostly to the MIH levels, as well as the desire to see light manufacturing uses in the ground floor of this building.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: City Planning Commission approved this unanimously as well.

chapters.summary: Cozen locates the site on the Williamsburg waterfront, one block from the waterfront on the west side of Kent Avenue, bounded by River Street, Metropolitan Avenue, and North 3rd Street. He notes proximity to the River Ring and Domino developments, describing the area as actively redeveloped.
chapters.title: Cozen describes 200 Kent Avenue site location and surrounding context

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: A brief overview of where the site is located.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: It's on the Williamsburg waterfront, one block in from the actual waterfront, on the west side of Kent Avenue, bounded by River Street, Metropolitan Avenue, and North 3rd Street.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: You might call this South Williamsburg, but it's kind of in an area that's being actively redeveloped by the potential River Ring project as well as Domino.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Just walking around the site, you can see in this image, looking to the northwest, the existing building is largely vacant.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: It was developed as a commercial office building with ground floor retail.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: There is a trade, as we'll see in an upcoming slide, there is a Trader Joe's in the ground floor.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: The second floor, which has a unique kind of metal cladding that was meant to screen a second floor parking structure that was to meet a

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: prior zoning requirement for parking here.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: As I mentioned, the Trader Joe's, which is at the corner of North 3rd and Kent, some additional retail fronts on Kent Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: But as I mentioned, the upper floors as well as the remaining retail ground floor has remained vacant.

chapters.summary: Cozen shows the existing largely vacant commercial office building with ground floor retail, including a Trader Joe's located mostly below grade. He notes the second floor has metal cladding screening a former parking structure, and upper floors and remaining retail have remained vacant.
chapters.title: Cozen describes existing building conditions including Trader Joe's

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: And I would just note, actually, that the Trader Joe's is most...

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: if not almost entirely located below grade.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: You can see here the office entrance on North 3rd Street, as well as two, a curb cut and two elevators that bring, that were intended to bring cars up to the second floor.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Moving around the site, we are looking at the building's River Street frontage and the Trader Joe's subgrade parking entrance, wherein 43 parking spaces are located.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Looking north here, I think this is the final photo.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: To the left is the currently vacant or used for commercial vehicle storage site where River Ring is intended to be developed.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: And we're looking north to the Austin Nichols building and the Williamsburg waterfront.

chapters.summary: Cozen describes the site's M1-4 district, originally modified in the 2005 Greenpoint-Williamsburg rezoning. He explains the patchwork of private rezonings that have created MX-8 districts, and the area's transformation from industrial to a vibrant mixed-use district with maker spaces, retail, and residential uses. He notes the height transition from waterfront to inland.
chapters.title: Cozen explains zoning history and land use context of Williamsburg waterfront area

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: The site, as I mentioned, is located in an M14 district.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: The zoning in this area was originally modified as part of the Greenpoint-Williamsburg rezoning back in 2005.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Since then, it's been somewhat of a patchwork where private actors, owners, operators have rezoned different pieces of property.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Those rezonings have mostly resulted in MX districts, MX8 in particular.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: And you can see here the varied land uses in the area.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: What was maybe 20, 30 years ago, a more industrial warehouse light manufacturing portion of the city has become

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: a pretty vibrant mixed-use district where maker spaces, commercial ground floor retail uses, as well as residential have begun to predominate.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: And here you can see the site relative to the built context.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: The site is located one block in from the Williamsburg waterfront, wherein along that waterfront there are some pretty sizable skyscrapers that have been built as well as proposed.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: City planning as well as the general built context here is to transition from the east to the west in terms of height and density.

chapters.summary: Cozen shows the proposed development: a partial conversion and enlargement of the existing building, approximately 22,000 square feet of development area, 93,000 residential square feet, retaining 43 parking spaces for Trader Joe's, with commercial ground floor and second floor. A rendering shows the building in context with the future River Ring towers.
chapters.title: Cozen presents proposed development and rendering for 200 Kent Avenue

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: And as I mentioned, this is just a representation of the zoning map change from an M14 to an M14AR7X district, which would allow for the future mixed-use development.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Next slide.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: On the screen right here, you can see the proposed development.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: The proposed development, we've added the river ring buildings just so you can see it in its built context in the future.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: The development area is approximately 22,000 and change in terms of square feet.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: And as I mentioned, the proposed development is a partial conversion of the existing building as well as an enlargement on top of that converted building.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: This would result in approximately 93,000 residential square feet.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: The proposed development would retain the 43 parking spaces for Trader Joe's in the basement and have commercial ground floor and on the second floor as well.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: And this is a rendering showing how the building will look with the river ring buildings to the west along the waterfront.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: We think it fits very nicely within the built context and would be a welcomed addition to the neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Next slide.

chapters.summary: Julio Magarino of Hill West describes the facade treatment: retaining existing ground floor storefront glazing with brushed aluminum, re-cladding the second floor with curtain wall and metal panels, re-cladding floors 3-5 with window wall systems, and designing floors 6-14 with alternating floor plates to create facade articulation and a unique identity for the developing neighborhood.
chapters.title: Architect Magarino presents building facade design and enlargement

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: So I'm going to turn it over to Julio, the architect from Hill West.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Julio, take it away.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: My name is Julio Magarino.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: I'm with Hill West Architect for the applicants.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: On this slide, as you can see, the proposed building will retain the existing ground floor storefront frame.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: Its glazing will remain.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: The existing metal panels will be replaced in kind with the brushed aluminum metal finish.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: The existing second floor currently has solid and perforated metal panels, which were constructed as screening as a previously required parking.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: The applicant proposes to re-clad the facade with a curtain wall and metal panels, same as the ground floor.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: The existing third through fifth floors currently have a current wall system.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: The applicant proposes to re-clad the facade with a window wall and metal panel system.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: The proposed enlargement floors 6 through 14 features two different floor plates that alternate and integrate with the existing structure below.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: These alternating floor plates combined with the slab cover metal panels accentuate the facade's articulation and create a unique identity for the project and its active developing neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Cozen wraps up the presentation, summarizing the M1-4A/R7X rezoning for mixed-use development with commercial ground floor and second floor, significant open space on the building's podium, and residential enlargement above. He emphasizes the project activates a mostly vacant commercial building and provides new market-rate and affordable housing.
chapters.title: Cozen summarizes proposed mixed-use program for 200 Kent Avenue

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Turn to Jeremy now.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Next slide.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: And just to wrap up, as I mentioned, the proposal here is to rezone the property to an M14AR7X district to allow for mixed-use development.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: As you can see on this slide, the ground floor and second floor, as proposed, will be commercial use.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: There will be a significant open space on top of the building's podium, and then the enlargement, which will be residential, will be built above.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: We think this is a great addition to the neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: It activates a mostly vacant commercial office building and provides new market rate and affordable housing for an area that is very much in desire.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Louis asks how the new addition will be constructed on the existing building and whether businesses will stay open. Cozen says Trader Joe's and other tenants will remain open during construction, staged across four frontages. He notes the building was structurally designed to support the proposed overbuild, making construction feasible with minimal disruption.
chapters.title: Chair Louis questions applicant on construction staging and keeping businesses open

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I have a couple of questions.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Could you discuss some more how the construction of the new addition will be achieved on the existing building and whether you plan to keep the existing businesses open during construction?

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: So this has been a discussion with the community as well as the council member.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: We can...

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: At current, we have had active conversations with Trader Joe's and the other tenants in the building, and they will remain open during construction.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Construction will be staged in a way, because we have four frontages, construction will be staged in a way to allow for those businesses to remain.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: open and to have as minimal disruption as possible.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: The unique aspect of this building was that even though it was built back when this was actually zoned M3-1, over six, seven years ago, five to seven years ago, it was

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: structurally it was built to support an overbuilt as we are proposing today.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: So someone very smart designed this building from a structural standpoint to allow for a future enlargement.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: So from a construction standpoint, excuse me, from a construction standpoint, the staging and the

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: From a staging perspective and a structural standpoint, we think this is doable and very feasible.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: They started to work for you in advance.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You're blessed.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I guess that's right.

chapters.summary: Louis asks when construction would start and how long it would take. Cozen estimates 2027 start assuming ULURP approval and DOB drawings, with architect Magarino estimating 12-15 months given the existing structure. Louis asks if the client intends to develop or resell; Cozen confirms the client fully intends to develop.
chapters.title: Chair Louis questions applicant on construction timeline and client intent to develop

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: When do you anticipate starting construction and how long do you anticipate the construction to take?

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: So in an ideal circumstance, 2027.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Construction commencement that assumes approval of the ULERP as well as the preparation of building buildings, DOB drawings.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Given that we're in July, it's fair to say 2027 is a fair assessment.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Given that, and I can turn this over to Julio to weigh in, I believe the construction timeframe would be truncated given the

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: the limited demolition required here, no excavation required here.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: So there are significant construction benefits to repurposing the existing building, including the podium that can assist with, you know, getting this to market as quickly as possible.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: That's correct.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: I think construction.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: On a building this size could be 18 to 24 months, being that a third of the building already exists.

utterances.speaker: Julio Magarino
utterances.text: It could be probably within 12 months to 15.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Does your client want to continue to develop or resell?

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Our client fully intends to develop this property.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Louis asks why a mixed-use manufacturing and residential district was chosen when the proposal doesn't include manufacturing uses. Cozen explains the MX district provides flexibility for non-residential uses, matches the predominant zoning in the area, and the applicant is committed to reaching out to local organizations to encourage light manufacturing tenants.
chapters.title: Chair Louis questions applicant on MX-8 mixed-use district selection rationale

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You're proposing to map a mixed-use manufacturing and residential district.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: However, your current proposal does not include manufacturing uses.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Why did you choose this mixed-use zoning district, and how does the district benefit your proposal?

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: It's a good question, and it's one we've been asked by the borough president's staff and the borough president, as well as the community board and Commissioner Osorio at the City Planning Commission.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: We are open to light manufacturing uses that are permitted pursuant to the MX district zoning.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: We are committed to reaching out to local organizations to encourage light manufacturing zoning here.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: The reason why it was selected as a zoning district was because that district allows for the green.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: flexibility of non-residential uses, as well as it is the predominant district in this area.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: So when you look at the neighborhood, you know, on a broader scale, and I don't know if we want to go back to that slide, but you can see that MX-8, the special mixed-use district, is

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: kind of a patchwork throughout this kind of area of Williamsburg.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: So it makes sense from a land use planning standpoint.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: It also makes sense from a flexibility of use standpoint.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Got it.

chapters.summary: Louis asks for the unit mix and affordability breakdown. Cozen says the building proposes up to 143 dwelling units with 29-43 affordable under MIH, with the applicant open to Options 1 and 2 and leaning toward Option 1 based on conversations with the council member.
chapters.title: Chair Louis questions applicant on unit mix and affordability breakdown

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And could you provide a unit mix and affordability breakdown for your residential units?

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: So it's this more detail is in our the applications racial equity report.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: I can tell you that as proposed, the building, based on certain dwelling unit factors, proposes to contain up to 143 dwelling units, of which 29 to 43 would be affordable pursuant to MIH.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: The applicant, I believe, is open to...

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Well, I shouldn't say I believe.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: The applicant is open to options one and two.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: There have been conversations with the council member about selecting option one.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Either option works with the development proposal, and we fully intend to develop in accordance with that option.

chapters.summary: Louis asks about the bedroom count. Cozen says the bedroom count hasn't been finalized as this is an illustrative building, but the racial equity report contains a standard studio/one/two/three-bedroom mix. He notes the community's desire for larger units and says the applicant would trend toward larger units, not all studios.
chapters.title: Chair Louis questions applicant on bedroom count

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And can you also give us the bedroom count?

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Given that this is an illustrative building, we have not developed that bedroom count at this point.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: There is an illustrative bedroom count in the racial equity report.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: What I would note is, and we heard this at the community board, there is a desire for this neighborhood to have larger units with more bedrooms.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: And we understand that desire, and we're looking to meet that.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: What is the applicant proposing?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Like, what is the applicant thinking about when it comes to bedroom count?

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Yeah, I think at this point it's a little early to weigh in on the bedroom count.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: I will say that we...

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: There is a standard studio one, two, three bedroom mix that's in that racial equity report.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: I would think of that somewhat as the floor and not the ceiling.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: And we would, if anything, trend towards larger units than smaller units.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: This is not going to be more units that are all studios.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: I don't imagine that's the applicant's intent.

chapters.summary: Louis relays Council Member Ressler's question about redesigning bike parking in the Trader Joe's parking lot for better and safer access, per the community board's request. Cozen says the applicant will use best efforts to work with Trader Joe's, noting the bike spaces are within Trader Joe's leased premises, and will incorporate community feedback.
chapters.title: Chair Louis relays Ressler's question on bike parking redesign at Trader Joe's

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Okay, I have a couple of questions by Councilmember Ressler because he had to step out.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The community board asks that you consider redesigning the bike parking in the Trader Joe's parking lot for better and safer access.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Will you make best efforts to fulfill that request?

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: I can say yes, the applicant does intend to work with Trader Joe's.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: I would just note that, and we've given this response to the community board as well as to the council member, and I think the council member is fully aware of this, the bike spaces are within the Trader Joe's leased premises.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: So the applicant fully intends to use best efforts to work with Trader Joe's to make sure that that space is better configured.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: including incorporating community feedback as to how we can make it safer and more accessible.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Consider that part of the feedback.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: They said they want it.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: All right.

chapters.summary: Louis relays Ressler's question about identifying light manufacturing tenants by working with Evergreen, North Brooklyn Business Exchange, and Greenpoint Manufacturing and Design Center; Cozen confirms the commitment. Louis asks about 485X; Cozen says it remains an open question whether 485X will be pursued given the partial conversion and enlargement nature of the project.
chapters.title: Chair Louis relays Ressler's questions on light manufacturing tenants and 485X tax abatement

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The borough president has asked that you identify light manufacturing tenants that can occupy the building's commercial spaces.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Will you make best effort to do that, including working proactively with Evergreen, North Brooklyn Business Exchange, and Greenpoint Manufacturing and Design Center to identify tenants?

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Yes, the applicant has made that commitment to the borough president and to the commissioner Osorio at the city planning commission.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Are you using 485X?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If so, does the project you propose already consider 485X?

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: At current?

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: Because this is a partial conversion and a partial enlargement, it is still an open question whether 485X will be, as a tax abatement program, will be looked to.

utterances.speaker: Jeremy Cozen
utterances.text: So we don't have that answer at this time.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Got it.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis calls for public testimony three times; no members of the public sign up in person or online. The hearing on LUs 115 and 116 is closed and items are laid over. The applicant panel is excused.
chapters.title: 200 Kent Avenue hearing closed: no public testimony, items laid over

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Council, are there any members of the public who wish to testify?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Remotely or in person regarding this proposal?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: No, there's no members of public signed up online or in person.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: For members of the public here, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If there are any members of the public who wish to testify regarding this proposal remotely, please press the raise hand button now.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If you are in person, please identify yourself to one of the sergeant at arms.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This is the last call.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You have 30 seconds.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Being there are no members of the public who wish to testify regarding LUs 115 and 116 relating to the 200 Kent Avenue mixed-use residential rezoning proposal, the public hearing is now closed.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: These items are now laid over.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This applicant panel is now excused.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you for being here today.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis thanks the public, colleagues, subcommittee council, land use staff, and sergeant at arms. The meeting is adjourned.
chapters.title: Chair Louis adjourns the subcommittee meeting

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: That concludes today's business.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I would like to thank the members of the public, my colleagues, subcommittee council, land use and council staff, and the sergeant at arms for participating in today's meeting.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This meeting is hereby adjourned.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thanks.

summary: Subcommittee on Zoning and Franchises held hearings on three land use items: Flatiron NoMad major concession expansion, 47-03 108th Street rezoning in Corona Queens, and 200 Kent Avenue rezoning in Williamsburg Brooklyn.

DOT presented the Flatiron NoMad major concession to expand the pedestrian plaza area from 38,500 to 162,345 square feet maintained by the Flatiron NoMad Partnership BID, with council members questioning oversight, environmental practices, public seating, bike-pedestrian separation, and concession seating limits.

Both rezoning applications propose mixed-use residential developments with mandatory inclusionary housing (119 units in Corona, 143 units in Williamsburg); all three items were laid over with no public testimony submitted.
title: Subcommittee on Zoning and Franchises
topic: Zoning, 200 Kent Avenue Rezoning, Brooklyn (C 260149 ZMK)
